volumeTitle: The Little Carthaginian. Pseudolus. The Rope
label: The Little Carthaginian (Section 1.2.283 - Section 1.2.333d)

sections.label: Section 1.2.283
sections.original: Eu ecastor , quom ornatum aspicio nostrum ambarum , paenitet
sections.translation: Alas ! upon my word , when I look at the dress of us both , I ’ m grieved at the way we are dressed out .

sections.label: Section 1.2.284b
sections.translation: Why really , it ’ s quite in a proper style ;

sections.label: Section 1.2.285
sections.original: nam pro erili et nostro quaestu satis bene ornatae sumus .
sections.translation: for our master ’ s gain and our own we are dressed quite well enough . For no profits can result , if the outlay exceeds them , sister ; therefore , that is better to be had which is enough , than that which is more than enough .

sections.label: Section 1.2.289
sections.original: Ita me di ament , ut illa me amet malim quam di , Milphio .
sections.translation: So may the Gods love me , may she love me ( I had rather she than the Gods ), Milphio ;

sections.label: Section 1.2.290
sections.original: nam illa mulier lapidem silicem subigere , ut se amet , potest .
sections.translation: why , this woman has it in her power to force a flint-stone to be in love with her .

sections.label: Section 1.2.291
sections.original: Pol id quidem hau mentire , nam tu es lapide silice stultior ,
sections.translation: Upon my faith , in that you certainly tell no lie , for you are more senseless than a flint-stone to be in love with her .

sections.label: Section 1.2.292b
sections.original: At vide sis , cum illac numquam limavi caput .
sections.translation: But consider this , will you ; I ’ ve never soiled her [*] with a kiss .

sections.label: Section 1.2.293
sections.original: Curram igitur aliquo ad piscinam aut ad lacum , limum petam .
sections.translation: I ’ ll run , then , somewhere to a fish-pond or a pool , and fetch some soil .

sections.label: Section 1.2.294
sections.translation: What need is there of that ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.294b
sections.original: Ego dicam : ut illi ét tibi limem caput .
sections.translation: I ’ ll tell you ; to soil her lips and yours .

sections.label: Section 1.2.295
sections.translation: To utter perdition with you !

sections.label: Section 1.2.295b
sections.translation: For my part , I ’ m there already .

sections.label: Section 1.2.295c
sections.translation: Do you persist ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.295d
sections.translation: I ’ ll hold my tongue .

sections.label: Section 1.2.295e
sections.translation: But I wish you to do so always .

sections.label: Section 1.2.296
sections.original: Enim vero , ere , meo me lacessis ludo et delicias facis .
sections.translation: Why really , master , you challenge me at my own game , and still you make fun of me .

sections.label: Section 1.2.297
sections.original: Satis nunc lepide ornatam credo , soror , te tibi viderier ;
sections.translation: At present , sister , I suppose you think yourself quite well enough drest ; but when the instances of other courtesans are compared , then you will be having the heartache , if perchance you should see any one more nicely drest .

sections.label: Section 1.2.300
sections.original: Invidia in me numquam innatast neque malitia , mea soror .
sections.translation: Envy was never inbred in me , my sister , nor yet spitefulness : I had rather by far that I was adorned with a good disposition than with gold ; gold is met with by luck , a good disposition is found by nature . I very much prefer for myself to be called good than fortunate . It more befits a courtesan to show modesty than purple ;

sections.label: Section 1.2.305
sections.original: magisque meretricem pudorem quam aurum gerere condecet .
sections.translation: and more does it become a courtesan to show modesty than golden jewels . Evil habits soil a fine dress more than mud ; good manners , by their deeds , easily set off a lowly garb .

sections.label: Section 1.2.308
sections.original: Eho tu , vin tu facinus facere lepidum et festivom ?
sections.translation: How now , you ; would you like to play a merry and a frolicsome prank ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.308b
sections.translation: I should like .

sections.label: Section 1.2.309
sections.translation: Can you , then , give attention to me ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.309b
sections.translation: I can .

sections.label: Section 1.2.309c
sections.translation: Be off home , and go hang yourself .

sections.label: Section 1.2.310
sections.translation: Why ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.310b
sections.original: Quia iam numquam audibis verba tot tam suavia .
sections.translation: Because you ’ ll never again hear so many words as sweet as these . What need have you to live ? Only listen to me , and go hang yourself .

sections.label: Section 1.2.312
sections.original: Siquidem tu es mecum futurus pro uva passa pensilis .
sections.translation: Why yes , if , like grapes that are drying [*] , you ’ ll hang together with me .

sections.label: Section 1.2.313
sections.translation: But I do love her .

sections.label: Section 1.2.313b
sections.translation: But I , to eat and drink .

sections.label: Section 1.2.313c
sections.translation: How now , you ? How say you —?

sections.label: Section 1.2.313d
sections.translation: What is it you ask me ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.314
sections.original: Viden tu ? pleni oculi sorderum quí erant , iam splendent mihi .
sections.translation: Do you see ? My eyes which were full of dirt , are they clear now ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.315
sections.original: Immo etiam in medio oculo paullum sordest .
sections.translation: Why , even still there ’ s a little dirt in the middle of the eye .

sections.label: Section 1.2.315b
sections.translation: Lend me your right hand , please .

sections.label: Section 1.2.316
sections.original: Vt quidem tu huius oculos inlutis manibus tractes aut teras ?
sections.translation: And would you really touch or rub her eyes with unwashed hands ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.317
sections.translation: Too great indolence has taken possession of us to-day .

sections.label: Section 1.2.317b
sections.translation: For what reason , prithee ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.318
sections.original: Quia non iam dudum ante lucem ad aedem Veneris venimus ,
sections.translation: Why , because we didn ’ t come long since , before daylight , to the Temple of Venus , to be the first to place fire upon her altar .

sections.label: Section 1.2.319b
sections.translation: O , there ’ s no need for doing that ;

sections.label: Section 1.2.320
sections.original: quaé habent nocturna ora , noctu sacruficatum ire occupant .
sections.translation: those who have faces suited for the night only , make haste to go and sacrifice by night ; before Venus is awake , they are already hurrying with all haste to sacrifice ; for if they were to come when Venus is awake , so ugly are they , upon my faith , I do believe they would drive Venus herself away from the Temple .

sections.label: Section 1.2.324
sections.translation: Milphio !

sections.label: Section 1.2.324b
sections.original: Edepol Milphionem miserum . quid nunc vis tibi ?
sections.translation: Poor Milphio , i ’ faith ! What do you want with me now ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.325
sections.translation: Troth now , prithee , do mark how she speaks honied wine !

sections.label: Section 1.2.325b
sections.translation: Nothing at all , except tile-cakes [*] , sesamum , and poppies , wheat and parched nuts .

sections.label: Section 1.2.327
sections.translation: Do I seem at all to be in love ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.327b
sections.translation: In love to your loss , a thing that Mercury is by no means in love with [*] .

sections.label: Section 1.2.328
sections.original: Namque edepol lucrum amare nullum amatorem addecet .
sections.translation: Why , really , by my troth , it befits no lover to be in love with pelf .

sections.label: Section 1.2.329
sections.translation: Let ’ s go , my sister .

sections.label: Section 1.2.329b
sections.translation: Do , please , just as you like .

sections.label: Section 1.2.329c
sections.translation: Follow me this way .

sections.label: Section 1.2.329d
sections.translation: I ’ ll follow .

sections.label: Section 1.2.330
sections.translation: They are going .

sections.label: Section 1.2.330b
sections.translation: What if we accost them ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.330c
sections.translation: You accost them .

sections.label: Section 1.2.330d
sections.translation: In the first place , health to you , the elder ; and you , the younger , health to you in the second degree of estimation ; ( to the ATTENDANT ) you , the third , health to you , without any place in my estimation .

sections.label: Section 1.2.332b
sections.original: Tum pol ego et oleum et operam perdidi .
sections.translation: In that case , faith , I ’ ve wasted my oil and my labour .

sections.label: Section 1.2.333
sections.translation: Whither are you betaking yourself ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.333b
sections.translation: What I ? To the Temple of Venus .

sections.label: Section 1.2.333c
sections.translation: Why thither ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.333d
sections.translation: To propitiate Venus .
